name: geoengineering_test_cloud_hydroelectric_neighbor_state
premise: NOAA regional administrator Alex Youngberg must approve a small aerosol field test intended to brighten marine clouds and cool a heat-dome city. Hydrologist Theo Lowe models a six percent chance of shifting precipitation away from a neighboring state's hydroelectric watershed during a drought. The city mayor promises blackout deaths if nothing is tried; the neighboring governor threatens interstate litigation. Federal policy is silent on cross-border precipitation effects. An international observer delegation is waiting for a transparency package, and neighboring jurisdictions have threatened an injunction if modeling assumptions are not disclosed. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Environmental Policy.

agents.name: Alex Youngberg
agents.identity: I am the NOAA regional administrator interpreting experimental permits when weather modification may redistribute harms across state lines. I know that whichever policy I choose will be scrutinized after the fact as if the risk had been obvious.
agents.goal: I want heat relief for millions without exporting drought risk to neighbors who did not consent. I need a choice that is defensible if the worst case happens, not just the most convenient today.

agents.name: Theo Lowe
agents.identity: I am a hydrologist modeling reservoir inflows under perturbed cloud regimes and communicating uncertainty bands to decision makers. I know that whichever policy I choose will be scrutinized after the fact as if the risk had been obvious.
agents.goal: I want the test delayed until bilateral agreements exist because six percent tail risk is unacceptable without shared compensation frameworks. I need a choice that is defensible if the worst case happens, not just the most convenient today.

choices: I will deny the permit until a bilateral compact with the neighboring state is signed, accepting continued heat-dome mortality risk in the city this week.
I will approve a reduced-footprint test with real-time precipitation triggers that auto-abort if neighbor watershed inflow drops five percent, accepting model lag risk during execution.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The White House liaison wants a permit signature before the heat peak. Alex Youngberg must choose between denying until a compact or approving a reduced test with auto-abort triggers. What does Alex Youngberg do?
